title: Maryland Crab Soup (Community Enhanced)
original_instructions: Place stewed tomatoes, 3 cups water, beef broth, lima beans, corn, sliced carrots, chopped onion, and Old Bay seasoning in a 4-quart pot. Bring to a simmer over medium heat; cover and cook for 5 minutes.
Bring 1 gallon water to a boil in a large pot. Add crab claws and boil for 6 minutes; drain.
Stir crabmeat and boiled crab claws into tomato and vegetable mixture. Cover and simmer for 10 to 15 minutes. Serve hot.
ingredients: 2 small bags Birdseye frozen veggies (green beans, corn, peas)
1/4 cup brown sugar and 1 tablespoon Worcestershire sauce
instructions: Place stewed tomatoes, 3 cups water, beef broth, lima beans, corn, sliced carrots, chopped onion, and Old Bay seasoning in a 4-quart pot. Bring to a simmer over medium heat; cover and cook for 5 minutes.
Bring 1 gallon water and 2 chicken bouillon cubes to a boil in a large pot. Add crab claws and boil for 6 minutes; drain.
Stir crabmeat and boiled crab claws into tomato and vegetable mixture. Cover and simmer for 10 to 15 minutes. Serve hot.

source_review.text: I am also from Maryland and although I made a number of adjustments, this is a very good basic recipe. Here is what changed: I used crushed tomatoes instead of stewed (28 oz can), used Birdseye frozen veggies, which has green beans, corn and peas (2 small bags), 1 1/2 cups cubed potatoes, 1/2 cup chopped savoy cabbage, 2 cans (not cups) of beef broth (low sodium), 1/4 cup brown sugar and 1 T. Worcheshire sauce. 3 Tablespoons of Old Bay was perfect for me, but for someone who isn't from the area, it might be a little too spicy. I cooked it much longer on low (about 2 hours), but I don't serve it until the next day so all of the flavors can develop.

modifications_applied.reasoning: Using crushed tomatoes instead of stewed and Birdseye frozen veggies adds convenience and variety

source_review.text: REVIEW: This was suuuuch a good soup! I started to dance knowing I was experiencing a piece of my childhood. I did make a few changes, for me, making it a richer soup. But, I was searching for my mom's kitchen when I realized the flavors were THAT close! Usually, we share meals during the week with my father who lives alone. This he didn't want to share!NOTES TO SELF: I tried to go heavy on the veggies for a more filling soup...found crabs claws at the International Store pretty cheap at the bottom of the live crab bin. (Be sure to boil separately as a film built in the water.) ... doubled the Old Bay for more spice...added chicken bouillon to the water for more depth (reduced salt in other areas due to added salt here)...ALSO ADDED THE FOLLOWING...2 potatoes... used crushed tomatoes instead of stewed... 2C chopped cabbage... 1C peas... 4 TBSP Worcestershire sauce... Red Pepper flakes to taste. Fantastic!!

modifications_applied.reasoning: Cooking the soup longer on low heat allows flavors to develop and intensify

changes_made.from_text: Bring 1 gallon water to a boil in a large pot. Add crab claws and boil for 6 minutes; drain.
changes_made.to_text: Bring 1 gallon water and 2 chicken bouillon cubes to a boil in a large pot. Add crab claws and boil for 6 minutes; drain.

source_review.text: I live in western PA. no fresh blue crab in my neighborhood. i did however have a package of imitation crab meat. I know all you marylanders are kringing lol. I made the recipe pretty much as written with that exception and a couple of others . I only had 1 can of stewed tomatoes so I used that and 1 can of diced and I had a can of Delallo's red clam sauce so I added that. {a little olive oil , garlic and basil never hurt anyone } It was great and I made a crab salad with peas and bacon with the other half of the package of "crabby meat". also great. I didn't have limas so added potatoes. I thought edamame would be a good substitute too.

modifications_applied.reasoning: Substituting imitation crab meat for fresh blue crab due to availability

enhancement_summary.expected_impact: Using crushed tomatoes instead of stewed and Birdseye frozen veggies adds convenience and variety; Adjusting ingredient quantities to personal preference and taste; Cooking the soup longer on low heat allows flavors to develop and intensify (and 1 more improvements)

description: This crab soup recipe is authentic Chesapeake Bay food made with Old Bay, lima beans, corn, carrots, and Maryland blue back-fin crab meat and claws.
